branchingScenario.title: PBL: Integration of Metabolism and Biological Oxidation — Clinical Reasoning

startScreen.startScreenTitle: PBL: Integration of Metabolism and Biological Oxidation
startScreen.startScreenSubtitle: Work through this clinical case to test your understanding of key concepts in metabolic integration and biological oxidation. Make diagnostic and management decisions based on the clinical scenario.

endScreens.endScreenSubtitle: You made the correct clinical decisions at each step. You demonstrated strong understanding of the underlying concepts. Well done!
You showed some understanding but chose a suboptimal path. Review the feedback provided and try again to reinforce your learning.
Your clinical reasoning needs strengthening in this area. Review the source material carefully, paying attention to the key concepts highlighted, then attempt the case again.

params.text:
Case: Suspicious House Fire

Firefighters rescue an unconscious 45-year-old man from a house fire. He has cherry-red skin colour. Despite high-flow oxygen, his condition deteriorates. Blood gas analysis shows a normal PaO2 but elevated lactate. The toxicology team suspects cyanide poisoning from burning synthetic materials.

Key concept: Cyanide blocks Complex IV of the electron transport chain, preventing oxygen from acting as the terminal electron acceptor. This halts oxidative phosphorylation despite adequate oxygen supply.

branchingQuestion.question: Cyanide poisoning blocks a specific complex in the electron transport chain. Which complex is affected and what is the consequence?

alternatives.text: Complex IV (cytochrome c oxidase) — prevents oxygen from accepting electrons, halting ATP synthesis

feedback.title: Correct!
feedback.subtitle: Cyanide binds to Complex IV, blocking the final step of the ETC where oxygen accepts electrons. This causes histotoxic hypoxia despite adequate O2.

alternatives.text: Complex I (NADH dehydrogenase) — blocks entry of electrons from NADH

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Complex I inhibitors include rotenone and barbiturates. Cyanide specifically targets Complex IV, the terminal oxidase.

alternatives.text: ATP synthase (Complex V) — directly blocks ATP production

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Oligomycin inhibits ATP synthase. Cyanide blocks Complex IV, preventing electron flow to oxygen. Without electron flow, the proton gradient cannot be maintained.

params.text:
Treatment Response

Hydroxocobalamin (vitamin B12a) is administered as a cyanide antidote. The patient's lactate begins to decrease. The team discusses how the cells had been unable to utilise oxygen for aerobic metabolism, forcing reliance on anaerobic glycolysis.

Concept: The three central metabolic hubs are Acetyl-CoA, pyruvate, and the TCA (Krebs) cycle. The chemiosmotic theory (Peter Mitchell) explains how the proton gradient drives ATP synthesis by ATP synthase.

branchingQuestion.question: According to the chemiosmotic theory, how does the electron transport chain generate ATP?

alternatives.text: The proton gradient across the inner mitochondrial membrane drives ATP synthesis by ATP synthase

feedback.title: Correct!
feedback.subtitle: Peter Mitchell's chemiosmotic theory: ETC complexes pump protons, creating a gradient. Protons flow back through ATP synthase, driving ATP synthesis.

alternatives.text: Substrate-level phosphorylation in the matrix directly produces all mitochondrial ATP

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Substrate-level phosphorylation produces only a small amount of ATP (in glycolysis and TCA cycle). The vast majority comes from oxidative phosphorylation via the chemiosmotic mechanism.

alternatives.text: Direct chemical coupling between NADH oxidation and ATP formation without a proton gradient

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: The chemiosmotic theory specifically disproved the chemical coupling hypothesis. ATP synthesis is driven by the electrochemical proton gradient.

params.text:
Clinical Resolution

The patient recovers with hydroxocobalamin treatment. His lactate normalises within 6 hours as mitochondrial function is restored.

Learning points:

  • Three metabolic hubs: Acetyl-CoA, pyruvate, and TCA cycle
  • Cyanide blocks Complex IV of the ETC
  • Chemiosmotic theory (Mitchell): proton gradient drives ATP synthase
  • Fasting: glucagon promotes fuel mobilisation; Fed state: insulin promotes storage
